author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
what is interesting, is that both wind power and my static thermal harvesting systems are deriving their power from air movement. 

The difference?  Wind is dependent on mass air particle movement in one singular direction. My method is dependent only on individual gas particle's motion, being available. Thus on the individual motion its constantly averaging at the speed of sound for the gas medium in question. As long as it has enough thermal energy to move, it generates power. the power generation though becomes a thermal net loss. so the more power you generate using my method the colder the generator gets as it cools down the gas it is using to generating power by siphoning potential out of it that leads to it having less thermal energy. Just need a path for environmental heat to escape from its higher thermal levels then to the lower colder gas particles to keep them energized :)

author: @edeniaAJ
text:
I can easily imagine a VAWT system where you essentially run your home off a battery bank and then trickle-charge that overnight with Wind turbines, when the energy isn't being used.

On a community scale however, I think we need a mix of Wind, PV, Solar thermal, compact hydro and tidal if you're near a river, and geothermal if you're in the right area.

author: @jakebruenderman2481
text: These are all good, but the best is a closed loop system hydro generator. Takes up about a cubic meter and will generate 240v at 8k Watts forever with minimal maintenance. But in the future, personal use nuclear will power our homes and cars. Rolls Royce already had it in motion.

text: Excellent Analysis! Thanks! I am poised to take my house off the grid. I am a retired electronics tech and plan to initially place a hundred vibration/leaf generators along the top of my garden fence this Summer, where there is almost always wind; also hungry deer who are  constantly plotting and planning to eat the garden! (The top edge of the fence with a hundred moving objects will be a great deterrent.)  Also plan to purchase evacuated tubes for hot water if I can find any these days. Batteries appear to be the limiting factor in a robust and powerful system, which I am not yet set up to manufacture -- yet. (I have to power my spa and steam room!) author: @TheSilmarillian
text: I live totally off grid here in Australia have been using solar for years just installed a small 4000W wind turbine its amazing the difference and it keeps my 24V battery system at peak at nights as we get a bit of wind here at night and in doing so will prolong the service life of my four new batteries, also added another four panels and probably another eight sometime this year .Before anyone asks my outlay to power a three bedroom house,workshop and outlying sheds about  6K,the batteries are still the most expensive part I just payed $1600 for four 120Amph delivered with an expected service life of eight years if maintained properly,so yes its doable.May I add I live remote and when I do go the coast I see houses with arrays of solar panels that are covered in dust and grime and I think is it so hard to get a hose and wash them down they will work better but I digress,I do that lol

author: @flightofthejellyfish
text: The answer is both. Wind and solar are highly complimentary; cloudy days tend to be windy, sunny days tend to be still. The neat thing about solar is that it produces it's power during the day when commerce and industry are in full swing - matches the demand curve quite nicely. Fair that's not the best if you are off-grid, yay producing lots of power while I'm off at work and the only thing running is the fridge, but on a larger scale quite good.

author: @earthlingthings
text:
I've used solar for 23 years now. The cost of the panels has fallen to 65£ per 100w. The only expensive part of the setup is the battery. Which both wind and solar require. By 12 noon my batteries get full. I charge up laptops phones etc by day freeing up night usage. Further, when I have excess by day I run my washing machine. And fridge. The latter I freeze ice blocks which cover the overnight downtime. All in all I can't complain. 

Having said that, I live in a wind rich location and am deeply interested in your Darwin design. Which you have perfected in separate parts, never the whole. For example you discovered having longer vertical sections on the serpentine was the best output. You discovered that magnetic bearings were the business. You discovered the Darwin wind redirector was the bees knees. You discovered the cone centred blades increased effeciency. That torroidal blades were the quietest. And finally, coil at the rim rather than centre was just awesome. And that gears were potentially a booster on top of all that.

Would you consider doing the updated version using all these aspects?

author: @shiningirisheyes
text: Solar presently north of middle France are not very effective .Example in spain a one meter square solar  panel give average of 5 kilowatts per day .Ireland for example the same solar panel give 2 kilowatts per day average . In reality in winter sod all power .Even in summer clouds and rain can for weeks reduce power to less than 10% of panels rated power  making solar in Ireland not great even hooked into battery soltions Problem is wind power in urban regions in Ireland is problematic making it mostly good for the large scale wind farms . Diesel or lpg generators looks to be north of 75 cents a kilowatt versus grid  wall sockets power cost of about 40 cents a kilowatt😢  Wood heat seems to be north of 100 cent per kilowatt .One UK long distance car commuter put a water tank in his car and collected waste exhast heat from engine and filled his house heating up with this hot water on his return home daving lots of heating costs .It could eork for some Irish and UK peoples .But for now  trapped into high cost grid  power in Ireland.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: If we can make the Darwin more prevalent that would eliminate a lot of the issues people have with Windmills. As far as solar, Efficiency is not the big issue. Battery storage is. Even if efficiency for solar is stuck @ 20% for the next 100 years. That doesn't really matter that much. The really important thing is actually "Cost" to make them, install them, maintain them and battery cost. The only real benefit to efficiency is simply how much space the panel takes up. And since they will be on Roof Tops, and Roof tops of most houses are almost always much much larger than the area needed to supply a home with electricity, space is almost never a problem and even if it was a problem, simply add panels. So, for solar... cost is the big issue.

author: @mircscreations6889
text:
In Australia there is a lot of support for Solar panel installations in the way of government rebates for solar panels and batteries but there is no rebates for wind generators Sad really.
You have hit the nail on the head with solar and wind generation its every individuals circumstances and environment that dictates what suits them. Having said that i also strongly support both wind and solar panel technologies if both are combined together it becomes a much more efficient solar system which operates 24/7 providing there is wind, as you all know in winter the winds pick up and there is a lot less sunshine for the solar panels due to clouds etc the wind generator can fill the gap of the losses from the solar panels. By putting in for example a 5kw solar array and a 5kw wind generator you will get around the 5kw average output all year round. I can give a little tip if you are buying a wind generator the more blades you have on it the more friction or wind resistance you will have and although it will start turning earlier with more blades however low revolutions don't produce lots of power you are much better off with a 3 blade wind generator with less wind resistance than a 5 or 6 blade. Remember most wind generators require them to spin at around 700 revolutions to produce their stated output of in our case 5000 watts. Hope this helps people out there thanks for your patience.

author: @enigma_7

author: @markmcd2780
text:
I have to disagree with the statement that there is very little research or investment in solar or wind. There are MASSIVE amounts of money being paid into both industries, both directly & in the form of subsidies.
Nobody would put solar on a home if the govt wasn't subsidising most of the actual costs.
Solar panel farms produce a massive heat island effect, altering the climatic environment across large areas.
Wind farms generate massive downstream alterations to airflow, altering the climate environment across large areas. If a butterly wing flapping can cause a storm on the other side of the planet, imagine what 300 metre turbine fins can do?

Both techs only make sense for each home to have their own supply - we just need storage to catch up & store decent amounts of energy & not burn things down.

author: @peterbrown172
text: Wind is useless in my area! We only get around4 windy months per year & they’re not consecutive. We are southern sub tropical. By the way, you live in a windy part of the world. It’s not a question of lifestyle when the wind doesn’t blow for days on end, then when it blows, you could fart harder! The wind does blow here in August to September then it’s back to the zephyrs again until April. In between, there are busters but they’re only hours and are capable of tearing up the light turbines that are needed to collect the gnat-farts wind we get most of the time. It’s so weak you can’t even get a kite up!

author: @sinenomine9093
text: An interesting observation, we're in the region of the US affected by the smoke from the Canadian wildfires, our solar electric production has held up quite well despite the haze.  I would estimate our solar electric production is only reduced by maybe 10-15%.  I think the reason is that the very noticeable haze reduces the direct solar irradiation but the panel still captures the indirect radiation due to scattered light.

author: @sinenomine9093
text:
It's not either, or, it's both.  At the moment, solar is really coming through for Texas during their heat wave.  Solar is literally keeping the Texas grid from crashing during the hottest part of the day.  Last winter, wind  did much the same during the coldest winter weather. In addition, both low-cost solar & wind reduced the need for as much high-cost natural gas during times of peak demand.
  Here, our grid-connected 9 kW solar panel provides more than enough electricity for our farm, house, and electric vehicle at less than 1/2 the price of retail power, 5 cents per kWh vs 12 cents retail.  We give the power company our excess production when we produce it, they give it back when we need it, night or day.

author: @VinoVeritas_
text:
Solar PV panels are less than £250 per kWp and will generate more than 20,000kWh over the 25 years of the warranty. Even after 25 years of zero maintenance, they'll still be generating ~85% of the power as they did on day 1.

Also, you don't need direct sunlight for solar PV to work. They only require light. Even in the UK, it's guaranteed that we'll get light every day

author: @leefoster4133
text: Fully disagree the way this seems to be going.  You need wind, solar, hydro, electro-static, etc.  The reason is when there is no sun there is no solar, when there is no wind there is no wind power, when there isn't enough water there is no hydro-electic and static will work all of the time but it's not going to generate enough power to make it worth it.  So, you need it all!  And that includes, coal, methane, nuclear and other power plants to generate the bulk of the power as green will never generate enough power all of the time to support an infrastructure.  Saying that it seems it all has to be commercial and it doesn't.  If I lived on the farm some where and I had both wind and solar then all of the extra power would be sent to the grid and that will lower the peaks of usage.  If Tom lives in say, Birmingham and has just solar then he too would add power to the grid and reduce the peaks.  The short of it is that for green energy to even remotely work is that it has to be community based where everyone does their bit to reduce the use on fossil fuels and nuclear but it will never remove it from being required.

author: @behappymike
text:
Interesting video Robert, I've been lucky enough to setup a  turbine and a small solar array in a rural area. Whilst i get solar only produces when the sun is shining, turbines only produce when the wind is blowing. 
For square foot of land took up, solar wins hands down as they can be installed on a roof of a structure making it multiple use land. The turbine needs stays so writes off a decent amount of area.
Also, unless you spend a lot on a turbine, breaking system, batteries, decent inverter etc you get very little out so pound for pound solar wins again. 
I'm sure this isn't the case everywhere, but this is just my experience based on my area 😊

Keep it up though! I have high hopes for your wind wall when you revisit it! 👍

replies.author: @behappymike
@ThatGuyChris11
replies.text: You can do a vertical axis turbine using a squirrel cage fan blade, can be installed anywhere just like a solar panel.

author: @grunwode
text: Panels are cheap as glass, even cheaper if you know how to replace a few burnt diodes.   It feels almost like one needs to take a whole semester class to understand why its hard for battery management systems to handle input from both wind and solar.  As interesting as it is though, I feel that microgeneration is a distraction from the more civilizationally critical task of interconnecting grids with bidirectional capacity.  Most people shouldn't need to learn how to become power plant operators.

author: @tomsimmons4482
text:
Another very interesting video, a couple of thoughts of my own.

The video without saying as much I believe is only discussing wind/solar for the purpose of electricity.  Whilst we don't all live in warm countries, even here is not so sunny Blighty, I think there is a lot that could be done to reduce your energy bills by using solar to heat your water.

When it comes to electricity, and focusing on domestic, I agree, solar isn't something you can really "tinker" with yourself, yeah, buy the panel, the charge controller and the battery and .. that's it.  

Wind and the the ability to construct through 3D printing, wood/metal work etc there is more scope.  However, there appears to be one fundamental problem, converting the electricity you've generated into electricity that you can actually use.  It is hard to integrate it into your existing house supply without expensive equipment.  You could add a wind circuit to your house that you could use to power things, but unless you add batteries and inverters (the expensive equipment again) it's not going to be mains voltage, in fact it looks to me the best you could achieve on a small/modest budget is a circuit that would USB sockets around the house to power/charge items.  I guess you could have a specific area, say a garage or shed, that you could power from batteries that are charges by wind, but only because that is an area that is used infrequently and for short periods of time, even then, you'd need a national grid backup in case you stayed too long.

Don't get me wrong, I watch all these videos, find them fascinating, I'm looking to use some of the ideas to power specific field applications, and I would dearly love to find a way of having vertical generators on my chimney stacks or similar that provided power for me to use that didn't incur a cost - but at the moment I can't see that is available.

author: @grendel1960a
text:
I agree wholeheartedly with you with regards to wind, however as you say it ll comes down to the location and application, for example I have a share in a boat, and we want an alternate off grid solution to keep the batteries topped up, currently she is plugged into shore power, but being a wooden boat they all leak a little, so the bilge pump runs occasionally, this means that if someone comes along and unplugs the shore power, the battery charger stops charging the batteries, and the bilge pump eventually flattens them. so we need a solution to charging the battery, my choice is a 50W monocrystaline flexible solar panel, this well even charge the batteries on a cloudy day (ok, not very well). the reason I didnt choose a wind system- well on the norfolk broads we have several low bridges, when 1" of clearance can mean the difference between getting under a bridge and not, vertical space is at a premium, so most wind is ruled out, unless mounted below roof level (where it will be shaded), thus the choice of a solar panel, as its flexible it can be mounted flat on the curved roof (and I lose maybe 1/4" clearance). the system I bought was a kit, including panel Charge controller and cables (£70 in total) as all it needs to replace is the battery charge to replace what the bilge pump uses, it doesnt mater that it doesnt run at night.
So the moral of the story is that wind is better, but circumstances may alter that view, as in this particular instance a solar panel does what is needed, hits the price point  and the application, without losing clearance under the bridges, Will I test a wind turbine? sure as Heck yes, but t would just end as another thing to have to lower to get under bridges

replies.author: @anthonycali6880
replies.text:
Hi, I purchased a 500W from controller. 
Unfortunately there's a high initial investment. I must have spent $700, which doesn't include installation. 
I only went to 20ft high, and am in a residential area. 
If, when the turbine turned, it gave its immediate power, I'd have appreciated it much more. However,  it does turn fairly well; but it must reach a wind speed to achieve greater than 10Vac (14.4Vdc), which then will charge my 12V battery. I have a solution.  I must design my own controller, and place a battery in series with wind turbine, which should start charging the solar battery. Because the series voltage would go into DC to DC converter, with Vin of 9 to 36V. Output say 15Vdc to charge battery, it would use 1/2 the energy from the series battery,  but I'm still trying to figure it all out.

replies.author: @dennisynborg6004
replies.text: Underwater tide turbines have great efficiency but... Anything that goes into the sea suffers from marine fouling, ie all marine life that clings to and grows on any available surface. This is an issue for ships for example since the marine fouling slows down the ship and increases fuel consumption. Thus the hulls are regularly cleaned and protective coatings applied. The same goes for turbines and this maintenance is higher and costlier than for wind turbines. There are other options though utilizing the sea for power generation and quite many experimental generators show great promise. I would like to see all those offshore wind turbine rigs to be coupled with wave energy extractors, thus the total install cost per megawatt would be decreased with hybrid/cogeneration solution.

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
It is possible to get a sort ot "solar" power at night, as long as it's a clear night, if you are using a Stirling setup... And maybe with a Stirling sitting off a panel setup but I don't think anyone's researched that yet.

The idea is at night, you use space as a heat sink and use that to cool the cool side of the Stirling while using excess heat or energy collected during the day, or ambient if it's hot enough, for the hot side.  

Only problem is it has to be completely clear over head. No trees, no wires, no clouds. 

Not sure what the output would be but folks have made ice with a solar funnel pointed at a clear sky

replies.author: @darrylgordon5170
replies.text: I come from an area that has a multitude of hydro generation facilities. Some that are centuries old. It is astonishing just how small of a trickle of water is required to do actual work.  in Rumford, Maine, there is in a park next to the Androscoggin River an overshot water wheel that runs on an absolute trickle of water.  I have turned off the water and let the wheel stop spinning, then turn the water back on. just to see how long it takes to get it running again.  The first bucket fills slowly, then spills over to the one below it. soon, the weight of the water is sufficient to move the wheel just enough to fill the bucket behind. then it begins to rotate. picking up speed as the weight of water overcomes inertia.
